items.title: Are Iran's retaliatory strikes across the Gulf region affecting the US' war strategy?
items.description: Israel's military said it began a broad wave of strikes on western Iran on Sunday. As the war progresses into the third week, further retaliatory strikes from Iran are likely to occur. So far, Iran has launched strikes across nine countries in the Gulf region. Most of these attacks have been intercepted but at least 19 people have been killed. The US and Israel started this war but it seems the Gulf states are bearing the brunt of Iran's retaliation. Could this affect the United States' strategy in the war? Analysis by Joe Macaron, geopolitical analyst and non-resident association researcher at the Arab World Institute in Paris.

items.title: Trump urges allies to send warships to Strait of Hormuz
items.description: President Donald Trump has threatened to get the Strait of Hormuz opened "one way or another" and has called on a number of countries including France, the United Kingdom and China to send ships to the area to overcome Iran's blockade on the vital trade artery. It comes as global oil supplies are skyrocketing and supply continues to be strangled with Iran blockading the strait and targeting the Gulf's energy infrastructure in retaliation to US strikes. Story by Shirli Sitbon and Eliza Herbert.

items.title: Trump administration threatens media outlets over Iran war coverage
items.description: The head of the Federal Communications Commission, which oversees US media outlets, has warned that some broadcasters risk losing their licenses over their news coverage of the Iran war. Brendan Carr – a Trump appointee – wrote in a social media post that broadcasters must operate in the public interest and those that are running hoaxes and fake news have the chance now to correct course before their license renewals come up. Free speech groups blasted Carr's warning, calling it outrageous. Analysis by Philip Turle, international affairs editor.

items.description: Escalation of the Iran conflict is sending shockwaves through global markets — driving up oil, fuel, and commodity prices, stoking inflation and recession risks worldwide. We hear from three NPR reporters in Europe, Asia and Russia.

items.title: South Sudan models chase global fashion dream despite visa hurdles

items.title: India news: Army says 'Pakistani terrorist' killed in Kashmir
items.description: The Indian army said the slain militant, who was killed after he opened fire, was found with "warlike stores." Meanwhile, the government has released new rules to manage the LPG shortage.

items.title: New rule changes confirmed for 2026 World Cup
items.description: FIFA and the International Football Association Board (IFAB) have announced several rule changes that will take effect at the 2026 FIFA World Cup, aimed at speeding up play and improving consistency in refereeing.

items.title: Toyota eyes lunar rover powered by regenerative fuel-cell tech
items.description: Toyota has teamed up with JAXA since 2019 to develop the manned lunar rover — which it dubbed the Lunar Cruiser — that they hope can be put on the moon in 2029.

items.title: Germany's Greens: More than leftist, woke ecologists?
items.description: Cem Özdemir won the election in Baden-Württemberg partly by abandoning many of his Green Party's core issues. His party is still debating whether this was a good move.

items.title: Netanyahu posts video in response to Iran rumours that he is dead

items.title: Chevron sees ‘more to be done’ by Milei to boost Argentina’s shale boom

items.title: Turnout low in French mayoral elections seen as key test ahead of 2027 presidential race
items.description: French voters cast ballots in municipal elections marred by low turnout on Sunday, in the last major test of the public mood ahead of next year’s all-important presidential election. The run-up to the vote was largely overshadowed by the Iran war and its fallout, notably the impact on fuel prices.

items.title: Iran says women's soccer captain drops Australia asylum bid
items.description: Five of the seven women who had been granted asylum in Australia have reportedly decided to return to Iran. Concerns about their safety arose after players remained silent during the Iranian national anthem.

items.title: Senegal makes $471 Million debt payment but faces tough times head
items.description: Senegal successfully paid nearly half a billion dollars in debt obligations on Friday, avoiding default, but spending cuts, delayed payments to other lenders, and growing civil unrest cast doubt on how much time the effort buys the West African nation.

items.title: Middle East war set to rage on as Iran's new leader breaks silence

items.title: Fritz Lang's 'Metropolis': The future is now
items.description: Lang's classic 1927 silent film, which was set in 2026, predicted a world that now looks eerily familiar, with a robot hinting at the future direction of AI.

items.title: 'Flying blind' farmers face three-year wait for new weather radar
items.description: Farmers in WA's north typically rely on real-time radar data for planting purposes during the wet season.

items.title: Pressure grows on Trump as Iran warns of long war that would 'destroy' world economy

items.title: What role is oil playing in the war in the Middle East and what's at stake?
items.description: More than two weeks ago, the United States and Israel launched attacks on Iran. Since then, Iran has retaliated against targets across the Gulf and effectively halted shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. Global oil prices have surged as a result. Did the United States factor this risk into its plans to strike Iran? How long is the spike in oil prices likely to last? FRANCE 24’s Alison Sargent asked Michael Bradshaw, professor of global energy at Warwick Business School in England.

items.title: France returns 'talking drum' looted in 1916 in Ivory Coast
items.description: France returned a sacred drum that was taken from the Ivory Coast during colonial rule at a ceremony in Abidjan on Friday, marking the first official restitution of a cultural artefact from France to the West African country.

items.title: Russia working to 'tip scale' for Orban in Hungary election
items.description: Viktor Orban has long been pro-Russia. Now, there are signs that the Kremlin is working to secure his reelection and turning Hungary’s election into a referendum on whether voters want to be closer to the EU or Russia.

items.title: Will Trump help or hinder Zimbabwe's white farmers in their compensation battle?
items.description: They have engaged a lobbying firm with close ties to the US president to argue their case.

items.description: Expats are torn between a desire to rebuild their homeland and preserving the hard-won prosperity they’ve achieved abroad, for themselves and their families. Leer más

items.title: Musk says Tesla's mega AI chip fab project to launch in seven days
Is Dubai's glossy image under threat? Not everyone thinks so
items.description: The city has been considered a safe haven by expats, tourists and influencers - but that's now being tested.

items.title: South Africa seizes four Chinese fishing vessels in Its waters
items.description: South Africa said it had seized four Chinese fishing vessels after they entered its Exclusive Economic Zone without authorization, triggering an inter-agency response.

items.title: Extreme heat and weather conditions attributed to stagnant jet stream
items.description: It’s no coincidence that extreme heat is engulfing huge swaths of Asia, Europe and North America all at the same time.

items.title: Amsterdam Jewish school targeted in attack
items.description: No injuries were reported in what Amsterdam's Mayor Femke Halsema described as a "deliberate attack." It follows an arson attack on a synagogue in Rotterdam a day earlier.

items.title: Boca Juniors unveils 80,000-capacity La Bombonera expansion plan
items.description: Riquelme says he has an initial permit from Ferrosur, the company responsible for the railway line that runs just metres from the Xeneize’s stadium, to embark upon the project. Leer más

items.title: Stories that caught our eye: March 6 to 13
items.description: A selection of the stories that caught our eye over the last seven days in Argentina. Leer más

items.title: Niger protest EU's resolution for unconditional relase of Bazoum
items.description: Niger's government summoned the European Union's chargé d'affaires in Niamey on Thursday to protest a European Parliament resolution calling for the "immediate" release of detained former President Mohamed Bazoum.

items.title: News24 | Israel strike kills 7 in Lebanon, Hezbollah announces major new operation
items.description: Lebanon said an Israeli strike on central Beirut’s seafront killed at least seven people, another attack in the heart of the capital as Iran-backed Hezbollah launched more missiles at Israel.

items.title: Cubans take to the streets and attack Communist Party office in rare riot over blackouts
items.description: Cubans took to the streets in the city of Moron on Saturday to protest worsening economic conditions since the US oil blockade. The island, known for harsh government repression, rarely sees protests. Authorities said five people were arrested in what it called vandalism acts. The NGO Justicia11, which monitors Cuban demonstrations, said gunfire was heard in the area and one person may have been shot. Story by Gabrielle Nadler.

items.title: Mother was 'living beyond her means' during fake cancer scam, court hears
items.description: An Adelaide mother who faked her six-year-old son's cancer diagnosis to deceitfully raise money was "living the life of the rich and famous" despite serious financial struggles, a court has heard.

items.title: Qld minister approved theatre name months before public consultation
items.description: Documents released under right to information show Queensland's arts minister was "set" on the name in January, months before the community was invited to have their say.

items.title: AFA treasurer Pablo Toviggino testifies in alleged tax evasion case
items.description: Pablo Toviggino appears before federal judge but declines to answer questions; AFA president Claudio 'Chiqui' Tapia due to testify on Thursday. Leer más

items.title: US eases oil sanctions at an ideal time for Russia
items.description: The United States has further eased sanctions on oil from Russia in an attempt to bring energy prices down as strikes on Iran continue. The news is a huge boost to the Kremlin, just when it needed one.

items.title: News24 | Nigeria’s new corridor opens doors for jihadist groups
items.description: Nigeria’s northwest and central border regions have become a hub for Sahelian militants, raising concerns about a widening insurgency corridor that could destabilise the region.

items.title: Australia will not send navy ship to Strait of Hormuz, minister says — as it happened
items.description: Australia will not be sending a navy ship to the Strait of Hormuz to protect oil-carrying vessels from Iranian attacks, the transport minister says.

items.title: Senegal soldier killed in clash during anti-cannabis operation in Casamance
items.description: A Senegalese soldier has been killed and six others wounded in the Casamance region during operations aimed at destroying cannabis fields near The Gambia, the army said on Thursday.

items.title: Pakistan says hit military facilities in Afghanistan
items.description: Another strike targeted a tunnel in Kandahar purportedly used by the Afghan Taliban and the Pakistani Taliban militant group or TTP, which Islamabad blames for a wave of strikes, they added.

items.title: Insta-savvy churches are propelling Brazil’s Gen Z to the right
items.description: From Instagram to TikTok, young Brazilians are posting Bible verses, attending religious services and proudly identifying as evangelical or Catholic. And with that is emerging a growing alignment with right-wing politics, imbuing a cohort that represents a third of the electorate. Leer más

items.title: Nearly 1,300 killed in Niger’s Tillabéri as jihadist attacks surge
items.description: In less than a decade, the Tillabéri region in western Niger has become the bloody frontline of a conflict between Niger and its neighbours against jihadist groups linked to Al-Qaeda and the Islamic State group.

items.title: China urges US to correct 'erroneous ways' over trade probes ahead of Paris talks
items.description: Beijing said on Monday it had “lodged representations” with Washington and urged the United States to “correct its erroneous ways” after new US trade probes launched last week. China’s commerce ministry called on Washington to meet Beijing halfway and resolve disputes through dialogue, as negotiators from both countries meet in Paris.

items.title: News24 | White House aides in tug-of-war battle for Trump ear over Iran war exit strategy debate
items.description: A complex tug-of-war inside the White House is driving US President Donald Trump’s shifting public statements on the course of the Iran war, as aides debate when and how to declare victory even as the conflict spreads across the Middle East.

items.title: A third of all Victorian car thefts now involve key cloning devices
items.description: Police say criminals are able to steal certain push-button start models within 15 seconds, using key reprogramming devices bought online.

items.title: Two brothers survive after Israeli troops kill family in occupied West Bank
items.description: Two Palestinian brothers are the only survivors after Israeli troops killed their parents and two siblings in Tammun.

items.title: Israel’s Foreign Minister denies reports of ‘direct’ Lebanon talks
items.description: Gideon Saar said that Israel sees “eye-to-eye” with ⁠the U.S. in the war with Iran, now in its 16th ‌day, and that the two allies are determined to continue until their goals are achieved

items.title: Catalan musician Joan Manuel Serrat says during Argentina trip that individualism leads ‘to the abyss’
items.description: Catalan singer-songwriter Joan Manuel Serrat warns in a speech while receiving honorary degree in Mendoza that individualism leads "to the abyss." Leer más

items.title: Hundreds of parties register for Haiti's first election in a decade
items.description: A record 280 political parties and counting had registered by Thursday’s deadline to participate in Haiti’s first general election in a decade, although not all will make the cut

items.title: Utilities surge kept Argentina's inflation rate at monthly 2.9% in February
items.description: Prices up 5.9% since turn of the year, 33.1% over past year; Housing, utilities and fuels, food and non-alcoholic beverages, miscellaneous goods and services recorded highest increases last month. Leer más

items.title: Europe repaid Tokyo a favour by supporting oil stock release, Japan minister says
France renews ties with CAR in first Ministerial visit in eight years
items.description: French Foreign Minister Jean-Noël Barrot arrived in Bangui on Friday for the first high-level French visit in eight years, declaring the "full restoration" of relations with the former colony as it navigates deepening ties with Russia.

items.title: Explainer: What does ‘No Quarter’ mean?
items.description: The prohibition against declaring “no quarter” is one of the oldest and most settled rules of war, codified in several major treaties, according to an expert

items.title: Observers note low turnout in Congo-Brazzaville presidential polls
items.description: The elections on Sunday are widely expected to ‌extend incumbent President Denis Sassou Nguesso's decades-long rule.

items.title: Oil rally can boost Argentina's shale industry, Vista CEO says
items.description: Higher oil prices stemming from Iran war in Iran could boost Argentina’s thriving shale patch, says Vista Energy CEO Miguel Galuccio. Leer más

items.title: From broker to bystander: Europe's Iran problem
items.description: Europe was once central to diplomacy with Iran, helping broker the 2015 nuclear deal. But the current crisis has exposed a less influential EU. Can the bloc still shape events or has it been reduced to the sidelines?

items.title: UN chief calls for ceasefire between Israel and Hezbollah
items.description: The UN Secretary-General António Guterres on Friday said the war in the Middle East, involving both Israel and Hezbollah, had caused immense suffering for the Lebanese people.

items.title: The Wall Street shuffle
items.description: President Milei cannot be faulted for his misfortune in timing Argentina Week in New York for what was Iran Week for the rest of the world and he obviously occupied centre stage, but sometimes what happens off-Broadway can be more decisive. Leer más

items.title: North Korea’s Kim Jong Un, daughter watch test of nuclear-capable multiple rocket launchers
items.description: Kim Jong Un oversaw the test on Saturday (March 14), which "involved twelve 600mm-calibre ultra-precision multiple rocket launchers and two artillery companies", the Korean Central News Agency (KCNA) said

items.title: Justified or not? US military families on fears of Iran war
items.description: BBC's North America editor Sarah Smith spoke to Gold Star mum Lynn, whose son died in the Iraq War, and veteran Jeremy.

items.title: Adorni is breaking his back
items.description: From his press conference pulpit, Adorni has lashed out against the caste, against Kirchnerism and anyone and anything that didn’t see the state as an obstacle to individual freedom, corrupting those who went near. How could a stalwart of Milei’s anarcho-libertarian gang abuse his position of power for personal privilege? Leer más

items.title: Cuban protesters ransack Communist office as energy crisis deepens
items.description: Discontent is mounting over rolling blackouts and shortages of food, fuel and medicine, exacerbated by a US blockade.

items.title: Who is running Iran? The regime's new strongmen
items.description: In the third week of war, who is leading the country? And to what extent have those still in place been destabilised and weakened by the US and Israeli strikes on the country? A veil of mystery surrounds a man who remains out of sight: Iran's new Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei. Another figure, Ali Larijani, meanwhile appeared centre stage at a pro-government demonstration.

items.title: Russia profits from Iran crisis as oil prices surge
items.description: Russia is earning nearly $588 million daily from fossil fuel exports in March—a 17 percent increase since February—as the Iran conflict disrupts Gulf oil supplies and drives prices higher, new analysis shows.

items.title: Boca Juniors planning bigger Bombonera – new stadium expansion plans leak
items.description: Venerable old home of the Xeneize is a daunting place to tread – and it may be getting even more so in the not so distant future. Leer más
